For Complainant: Megan J. Harris and Amy Tai, Attorneys, Office of the Solicitor, U.S. Department of Labor, New York, NY. For Respondent: Jeremy K. Fisher and Amy Conley, Jackson Lewis, P.C., Atlanta, GA.
Travis Heim, a DISH field technician; Brandon Barrett, his supervisor and DISH's field service manager.
Ashley Irwin and Lydia Calderon.

September 7, 2022.

12:16 p.m.; 14 minutes elapsed between the first photograph at the restaurant and her entering the worksite.
A 2-day trial in Syracuse, New York.
Irwin's field notes (the first photograph was time-stamped at 12:16 p.m.; according to Irwin's field notes only 14 minutes elapsed between that photograph and her entering the worksite).

(a) the applicability of the cited standard; (b) the employer's noncompliance with the standard's terms; (c) employee access to the violative conditions; (d) the employer's actual or constructive knowledge of the violation. Cited from Jake's Fireworks Inc. v. Sec'y of Labor, 893 F.3d 1248, 1256 (10th Cir. 2018) (citing Atlantic Battery Co., 16 BNA OSHC 2131, 2138 (No. 90-1747, 1994)).

29 C.F.R. § 1926.501(b)(1); it mandates protection for each employee on a walking/working surface with an unprotected side or edge which is 6 feet (1.8 m) or more above a lower level.
Austin Bldg. Co. v. Sec'y of Labor, 647 F.2d 1063, 1067-68 (10th Cir. 1981); and New York State Elec. & Gas Corp. v. Sec'y of Labor, 88 F.3d 98, 105 (2d Cir. 1996).
Rope: one-inch diameter, grey, 25 feet long. D-ring: in the center back of the harness, about four to six inches in diameter.
$124,305.00 proposed penalty; willful citation issued under the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970, 29 U.S.C. §§ 651-678.

She admitted she never attempted to interview Heim, never asked him where the anchor point was located on the roof, never asked whether he had worked on the roof without fall protection, never asked Barrett if he knew whether Heim was tied off, and never climbed the ladder herself to personally observe whether Heim was tied off.

The Court finds Barrett's comments about the company's policy of firing employees for fall protection violations were not an admission that he knew Heim had been working without fall protection. The evidence shows Barrett had no reason to believe, prior to meeting Irwin, that Heim was working without fall protection.

Despite Irwin's testimony that she could see the D-ring on Heim's back from her vantage point inside the restaurant, the D-ring is not visible in the photographs she took from that same vantage point. The photographs show a front or side view of Heim, not the middle of his back where the D-ring is located.

The record reflects DISH's safety program, training, and supervision were robust; DISH had no reason to believe Heim required continuous monitoring for use of fall protection; reasonable diligence did not require DISH to continuously verify Heim was following its safety policy. The Court concludes the Secretary did not prove a lack of reasonable diligence and thus did not prove constructive knowledge. And even if it was, the Court concludes the conduct was not foreseeable.

The worksite description establishes that Heim's D-ring was in the center back of the harness (four to six inches in diameter). The analysis observes that the photographs from inside the restaurant show only a front or side view of Heim, not the middle of his back, and that the D-ring is not visible in those photographs. Because Irwin testified she could see the D-ring on Heim's back from inside the restaurant but the D-ring is not visible in the photographs she took from that vantage point, the Court does not credit her testimony.

The Court concludes the Secretary failed to prove (1) that the cited standard was violated and (2) that DISH had knowledge of the violative condition. Accordingly, IT IS HEREBY ORDERED THAT the citation is VACATED.
